upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a near-saturated low-level atmosphere with a near-zero temp/dewpoint spread at 850mb (1482m MSL), indicating a moist, stable layer capping thermals in the morning. The surface-to-850mb lapse rate of 6.63°C/km is near moist adiabatic, suggesting weak but possible afternoon convective development as the boundary layer heats. Thermal ceiling is modest — BL heights in the models top out around 970–1170m AGL at Ed Levin and similar elevations inland, and upper winds at 700mb are light (11 kts from 235°) with no significant shear threat.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. KSFO reporting FEW017–SCT037, wind 290–300° at 7–8 kts — typical marine influence. KHAF calm and saturated (temp/dewpoint spread 0°C) with SCT–BKN 034–060 through mid-morning, indicating a shallow marine layer at the coast that is slowly lifting. KRHV calm with SCT018 BKN030 at 1447Z — low cloud deck over the South Bay inland valley. KAPC calm and foggy with FEW055 — Napa valley suppressed, consistent with stable overnight air draining pattern. KWVI data absent. All METARs showing calm surface winds as of ~1455Z, confirming the pre-sea-breeze lull.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft launch)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge, NW-facing high launch
sites.howItWorks: High launch uses thermally driven lift off the east Bay hills and a developing NW valley breeze. Best in NW–NNW flow 8–18 kts; thermals build from mid-morning and peak early-to-mid afternoon before the sea breeze strengthens.
sites.todaySetup: The deciding factor today is a morning cloud deck and weak early winds, but conditions improve meaningfully through midday and afternoon. KRHV is calm with SCT018 BKN030 at launch time, meaning the valley is suppressed under low cloud that should break by 10–11 AM based on the HRRR showing 51% cloud cover at 1750 ft by 1000Z. Once the cloud clears, BL heights climb to 830–970m AGL by 1 PM with CAPE around 150–170 J/kg — enough for workable thermals. Winds at 10m build from 5–7 kts NNW at 11 AM to 7–8 kts NNW by 2 PM, staying within a comfortable range for the high launch. By 5–6 PM winds accelerate to 11+ kts at 10m (13–15 kts at 80m), which is the upper edge for relaxed flying — the afternoon sea breeze arrival tightens the window. Tomorrow.io shows lighter surface winds (2–5 kts) through the afternoon at the ridge with gusts only to 8 kts, suggesting the HRRR numbers may be the upper bound; either way, the thermal window mid-morning to early afternoon looks solid. The main risk is the morning cloud delay and an accelerating NNW sea breeze after 4 PM.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Overcast to mostly cloudy with calm to 6 kt NNW winds; BL only 530–830m. Cloud deck suppresses thermals — not yet ready at high launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Cloud breaks to 40–65%, BL climbs to 830–970m with CAPE 150–170 J/kg. Winds NNW 6–7 kts at surface, 7–8 kts at 80m — ideal thermal window, smooth and manageable.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 8–11 kts NNW by 3–4 PM and 11+ kts by 5 PM; BL begins collapsing after 5 PM. Good soaring until ~4 PM, then increasingly brisk — wrap up before 5 PM.

verdict.hazards: Morning cloud delay; accelerating NNW sea breeze after 4 PM; winds at 80m hit 13 kts by 5 PM

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft launch)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower ridge launch, NW-facing, lower LZ approach
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch is more sheltered and works on lighter thermal days; it shares the same valley breeze cycle but is lower and more forgiving. Best with NW 5–12 kts and developing thermals.
sites.todaySetup: Conditions at the 600 ft launch mirror the high launch but are slightly more forgiving due to lower exposure. The morning cloud deck (SCT018 BKN030 at KRHV) keeps thermals suppressed until roughly 10–11 AM, at which point the BL expands to 885–990m AGL and clouds thin to 47–58%. Winds are light NNW 4–7 kts through midday — gentle and within easy P2 range. BL heights peak around 1170m AGL at 1 PM with CAPE 160 J/kg, indicating workable thermals. The afternoon sea breeze builds winds to 9–11 kts by 3–4 PM and 11–13 kts by 5–6 PM, which is still manageable from the lower launch but demands attention. Tomorrow.io shows gusts only to 7–8 kts at this elevation, confirming a mellow day. The 600 ft is a good option for P2 pilots wanting a calmer version of today's window.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: BKN–OVC low cloud with calm winds; BL only 455–885m. Thermal development suppressed — wait for cloud to break.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Cloud thins to 46–74%, BL 990–1100m, CAPE 160–170 J/kg. NNW winds 5–7 kts — solid thermal window, gentle and accessible for P2.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds increase to 8–9 kts by 3 PM, 11–12 kts by 5 PM. BL starts to collapse after 5 PM. Good flying until ~4:30 PM; monitor for gusty sea breeze push.

verdict.hazards: Morning cloud deck delays start; late afternoon sea breeze builds to 11–13 kts; gusts modest per Tomorrow.io

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W-facing ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site driven by the Pacific sea breeze hitting the coastal bluff. Ideal wind is W–NW at 12–22 kts; too light means no lift, too strong means rotor and turbulence below the cliff.
sites.todaySetup: The morning window is the critical concern here: KHAF is reporting calm winds with 0-knot surface winds and near-saturated air (temp/dewpoint 9–10°C/9–10°C) as of 1455Z, indicating the marine layer is sitting right at the coast with no established sea breeze yet. The SF Bar buoy (46026) shows only 7.8 kts from the north — offshore flow, not onshore. GFS and HRRR both show winds building very slowly through the morning, only reaching 5–8 kts W–NW by noon and 10–11 kts by 3–4 PM. This is a light day for Mussel Rock — marginal ridge lift territory. Tomorrow.io diverges significantly, showing the wind staying light and from the NE–N (33–37°) through mid-afternoon, which would be cross-tail to the W-facing bluff and unlandable. If Tomorrow.io is correct, the site is a no-fly for much of the day. HRRR shows more typical W–NW flow arriving by afternoon (308° at 10 kts by 3 PM), but even that is on the lighter side. The safest window would be 2–5 PM if the W–NW sea breeze establishes, but it may never get strong enough for reliable ridge lift. Wave height of 1.9m at 10-second period from HMB buoy (SST 13.9°C, cold) suggests a deepening marine layer that could delay burn-off and keep coastal winds light longer. P3+ pilots willing to wait and monitor on-site; not a reliable day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: KHAF calm with saturated air and SCT–BKN cloud; offshore buoy shows N flow. No sea breeze established — site is dead, do not launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Models diverge: HRRR shows W–NW 5–8 kts building slowly; Tomorrow.io shows NE–N 4–5 kts (cross/tail). Marginal at best — monitor on-site before committing.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: HRRR shows W–NW 10–13 kts by 3–4 PM; if this materializes it's the only viable window. Tomorrow.io remains skeptical with lighter, more variable flow. Gusts could be punchy if sea breeze arrives late and compressed.

verdict.bestWindow: 2 PM – 5 PM (if W–NW sea breeze arrives)
verdict.hazards: Model disagreement on wind direction; Tomorrow.io shows N–NE cross/tail flow through midday; cold SST and deep marine layer may suppress sea breeze all day; rotor if wind arrives suddenly strong

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge, NW-facing, thermal and ridge hybrid
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak soars on NW valley breeze and afternoon thermals off the south-facing slopes. Best in NW 8–18 kts with developing thermals; the peak channels the sea breeze well once it arrives from the Bay.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak shares the same morning suppression story as Ed Levin: KRHV is calm with SCT018 BKN030, meaning the south Bay valley is under low cloud that delays thermal development. The BL heights and CAPE values are nearly identical to Ed Levin — BL reaching 830–970m AGL with CAPE 150–170 J/kg by midday, suggesting a usable thermal window from roughly 11 AM. Winds build from 6–7 kts NNW at 11 AM to 7–8 kts at 2 PM and 8–9 kts by 3 PM, staying in a comfortable range. By 5 PM winds hit 11 kts at 10m and 13 kts at 80m — the upper limit. Tomorrow.io is notably lighter (1–5 kts) through the afternoon with gusts only to 7 kts, suggesting the true wind may be gentler than HRRR implies; either way, the thermal window looks workable. The site is very similar to Ed Levin high launch in character today. One note: the upper-air wind at 700mb comes from 235° (WSW) at 11 kts — this could introduce some cross-wind influence at altitude for longer XC flights, worth monitoring.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Overcast with calm winds at KRHV; BL suppressed to 530–830m. Wait for cloud to break — no viable window yet.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Cloud breaks to 40–65%, BL 825–970m, CAPE 130–170 J/kg. NNW 6–7 kts — the prime window; smooth thermals and comfortable wind.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds accelerate to 9–11 kts NNW by 3–4 PM and 11+ kts by 5 PM. Good soaring until ~4 PM; increasing sea breeze push after that narrows the comfort window for P2.

verdict.hazards: Morning cloud delay; 700mb WSW wind may affect upper-level XC tracking; sea breeze acceleration after 4 PM; BL collapses after 5 PM

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday looks more complex. GFS and HRRR both show 100% cloud cover all day with W–WNW winds at 8–9 kts at launch and BL heights capped at 390–475m. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 14 kts by afternoon with very low visibility (2 km) at 1400–1600 local — likely fog or low stratus pushing inland. Thermal development appears suppressed all day. Currently shaping up as a marginal-to-no-fly day at Ed Levin.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Same pattern as high launch — overcast all day, BL capped near 415–565m, westerly flow 7–9 kts. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 12 kts and visibility dropping to 2 km in the afternoon. Low ceiling and poor visibility make this a likely no-fly for meaningful soaring.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday looks much stronger at Mussel Rock — GFS/HRRR show W–NW winds 10–13 kts at 10m and 12–18 kts at 80m all day, with BL heights 670–905m. Tomorrow.io corroborates with 13–17 kts and gusts to 19–22 kts, which is excellent ridge lift but potentially strong-to-overdone by midday. P3+ pilots who enjoy powered ridge sessions should watch this carefully.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday mirrors Ed Levin — 100% cloud cover all day, W–WNW winds, BL capped at 390–475m. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 14 kts and visibility 2–10 km. Thermals suppressed; not a viable thermal flying day. Ridge soaring not applicable here.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Wednesday (Apr 15) shows a similar morning cloud deck (100% through noon) but BL heights improve significantly in the afternoon — 890–1210m AGL by 2 PM with light NW 6–9 kts. Cloud cover drops to 56% by 4 PM. This looks like a delayed but potentially flyable afternoon window, similar to today but with a later start. Worth monitoring.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Same as high launch on Wednesday — afternoon clearing with BL to 1020–1210m and NW 7–9 kts. Cloud cover persists through noon then breaks. A viable late-afternoon window may emerge around 2–5 PM.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Wednesday shows W–NW winds continuing strong at 13–15 kts with 100% cloud cover all day. Another ridge day but with persistent overcast; visibility and ceiling will determine if it's flyable. Winds look reliable for ridge work.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Wednesday shows morning cloud clearing more slowly — 100% through noon, then 89% by 2 PM. Winds shift more southerly (257–288°) at 80m, which may not be ideal for NW-facing launch. BL reaches 960–1180m in the afternoon. A marginal window may exist but wind direction warrants closer review.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 data not available beyond April 15 in current dataset. Based on the pattern, a gradual improvement in thermal conditions is expected as the inland heating cycle reasserts itself, pending any new weather systems.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 data mirrors high launch pattern — trend toward improving afternoon conditions if the marine layer continues to retreat through the week.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Day 3 not in dataset. If the W–NW pattern holds, Mussel Rock may continue to see reliable ridge conditions, though overcast may persist. Monitor evolving pressure gradient.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Day 3 data not available. Wind direction shift toward SW seen on Day 2 afternoon bears watching — if this continues into Day 3, Mission Peak's NW-facing launch may become less favorable.

watchlist: Model disagreement at Mussel Rock: Tomorrow.io shows N–NE (cross/tail) flow while HRRR shows W–NW — verify on-site before launching
Morning low cloud deck (SCT018 BKN030 at KRHV, BKN034–OVC055 at KHAF) delays thermal start at all inland sites — do not rush to launch early
Cold SST at Half Moon Bay (13.9°C) with 10-second wave period indicates a deep, persistent marine layer — coastal burn-off may be slow all week
Tuesday forecast shows potential fog/stratus push inland with visibility dropping to 2 km at Ed Levin per Tomorrow.io — may cancel Tuesday flying
Sea breeze acceleration after 4 PM today at inland sites — plan to land before 5 PM
tldr: Today's best flying is at Ed Levin (either launch) and Mission Peak, with a solid window from 11 AM to 4 PM once the morning cloud deck clears — light NNW 6–8 kts and moderate thermals to ~1000m AGL. Mussel Rock is a gamble today due to model disagreement on wind direction and a slow marine layer; locals-only until the sea breeze establishes. Get up early to watch the cloud burn-off, and be on the ground before the sea breeze cranks past 4 PM.
